questions.question: Explain why use case diagrams alone are insufficient for complete system specification and what supplementary models are needed.
keyPoints: Use case diagrams only show high-level functionality ('what')
They don't specify internal workings or object interactions
Need activity diagrams for workflow/flow of control
Need sequence/collaboration diagrams for object interactions
Need class diagrams for structural view
Need state diagrams for object behavior

questions.question: Describe the three types of objects identified in sequence diagrams and their roles.
keyPoints: Boundary objects - handle interface/interaction with actors
Entity objects - represent business data/information
Control objects - coordinate behavior/manage other objects
Each type has distinct responsibility in use case realization

questions.question: Differentiate between sequence diagrams and collaboration diagrams with respect to their primary focus.
keyPoints: Sequence diagrams emphasize temporal ordering (time)
Collaboration diagrams emphasize object organization/relationships
Sequence shows message flow chronologically
Collaboration shows object structure and links
Each serves different design purposes

questions.question: When is it appropriate to use state diagrams in system modeling? Give an example.
keyPoints: Use when object behavior depends on its state
Suitable for objects with finite number of states
Examples: Order lifecycle, User account status, ATM states
Shows states, transitions, events causing transitions
Not needed for simple stateless objects

questions.question: Explain the purpose of swimlanes in activity diagrams and how they improve the model.
keyPoints: Swimlanes partition activities by responsible entity
Can represent actors, departments, or system components
Improves clarity by showing WHO does WHAT
Helps in assigning responsibilities
Makes workflow easier to understand and analyze

questions.question: For a banking system 'Withdraw Cash' use case, draw/explain what sequence diagram elements would be needed. Include objects, messages, and lifelines.
keyPoints: Identify boundary objects: ATMInterface, Screen
Identify entity objects: Account, Card, Transaction
Identify control objects: WithdrawalController
Show messages: InsertCard, Validate, EnterPIN, CheckBalance, DispenseCash, etc.
Show activation bars and return messages
Include alt/loop fragments for different scenarios
Lifelines showing object existence during interaction

questions.question: Explain how activity diagrams supplement use case diagrams. Describe a scenario where an activity diagram would be more appropriate than a sequence diagram.
keyPoints: Activity diagrams show flow of control/activities
Use cases show functional requirements at high level
Activity diagrams elaborate the workflow within a use case
Example: Order Processing use case - activity diagram shows steps: Receive order, Check inventory, Process payment, Ship order, Notify customer
Sequence diagram better for showing object interactions chronologically
Activity diagram better for business processes with decisions and parallel paths
Activity can show multiple actors and their responsibilities via swimlanes

questions.question: Describe various supplementary UML models and explain when each should be used during system analysis and design.
keyPoints: Activity diagrams - for business workflows and process flows
Sequence diagrams - for object interactions over time
Collaboration diagrams - for object relationships and organization
State diagrams - for object behavior dependent on state
Class diagrams - for structural view showing classes and relationships
Component diagrams - for physical software structure and dependencies
Deployment diagrams - for hardware-software mapping
Guidelines: Use sequence for dynamic interaction, state for reactive objects, activity for processes
